key_departures.impact: Dalman had signed a 3-year, $42M deal last offseason and earned a Pro Bowl nod as the anchor of Chicago's offensive line. His sudden retirement created a gaping hole at center and was the Bears' most pressing pre-free-agency need. The Bears traded a 2027 fifth-round pick to New England for Garrett Bradbury as a direct replacement.

Moore was traded to the Bills along with a 2026 fifth-round pick in exchange for a 2026 second-round pick (No. 60 overall). He was Chicago's most experienced WR after three seasons. His departure thins the receiver room significantly, though Rome Odunze and Luther Burden III are expected to step into larger roles.

Edmunds was released by the Bears (providing ~$15M in cap relief) and signed a 3-year, $36M deal with the Giants. His departure left a starting hole at LB alongside an already-injured T.J. Edwards. The Bears moved quickly to address this with Bush and re-signing Jackson.

2025 First-Team All-Pro who led the NFL with 7 interceptions. Entering free agency with no reported deal back to Chicago. Reports indicate the Bears will not re-sign him. His departure is the largest talent loss of the offseason.

Second starting safety hitting free agency simultaneously with Byard. The Bears could not afford both. As of this writing, Brisker appears to be moving on, with Dallas linked as a possible destination. His departure — along with Byard's — left Chicago without any returning safety starters.
Wright had a breakout Pro Bowl season in 2025 (5 INTs, 11 PDs) and signed a 1-year, $5.5M deal with the Jets. His departure removes a reliable starter from the CB depth chart, escalating the CB need.

Zaccheaus departed for Atlanta after one season in Chicago. He finished with 30 catches for 315 yards but had lost snaps to Luther Burden and dealt with drops. His role as WR3/4 depth will need to be filled.
Robinson signed a 1-year, up-to-$4M deal with Houston. He never became a consistent pass rusher but was a solid rotational DE and run defender. Reduces EDGE depth behind Montez Sweat.
Billings hit free agency as the Bears' nose tackle starter. His departure directly triggered the Gallimore signing. Still unsigned as of this writing.
Released to clear ~$2M in cap space. Depth loss at LB in an already thin room.

contract.notes: Inherited contract; cap hit $5.7M in 2026 per reports

signings.analysis: Acquired via trade after Drew Dalman's shocking retirement. Bradbury is experienced, durable, and has familiarity with developing young QBs (Drake Maye). His $5.7M cap hit is cap-friendly relative to Dalman's deal. However, he is a clear step down from Dalman in quality — he is a bridge/stop-gap solution rather than a long-term starter. The Bears likely remain interested in upgrading IOL via the draft. IOL need drops from critical to moderate.

The Bears' biggest splash of early free agency. Bryant is 26, a Super Bowl champion, and recorded 4 INTs in 2025. The 3-year deal at $25.75M guaranteed signals a real investment — he is the new S1. However, with both Byard and Brisker expected to depart, the Bears still need a second starting-caliber safety. S need drops from critical to moderate — one spot solved, one still open.

Bush had a career-best 2025: 125 tackles, 3 INTs (2 pick-sixes), 2 forced fumbles. The 3-year/$21M guaranteed deal is a real commitment that replaces the released Tremaine Edmunds. Combined with D'Marco Jackson's re-signing, the Bears now have two credible starters at LB. T.J. Edwards (recovering from fractured fibula) remains uncertain, so some depth concern lingers, but the starting corps is solidified. LB drops from critical to a tier 3 depth need.

contract.notes: Guarantees estimated at $5M; exact figure TBD per reports

signings.analysis: Gallimore had 3.5 sacks and 38 tackles with the Colts in 2025 in a rotational role. He is the new nose tackle to replace Andrew Billings and should aid Gervon Dexter and Shemar Turner. However, he has never been a regular starter (rarely exceeded 56% snap share with Indy) and is on his fourth team in four years. The 2-year deal at below-starter AAV signals rotational depth, not a long-term starter. IDL need is partially reduced but not solved — still a prime draft target.

contract.notes: Up to $10.5M with incentives; base value ~$7.5M per multiple reports; guarantees estimated

signings.analysis: Jackson earned NFC Defensive Player of the Week twice in 2025 after stepping in for Edmunds. He is now in line to start opposite T.J. Edwards in Dennis Allen's defense. The below-market deal (cap hit ~$3M in 2026) reflects his unproven track record but his retention was important given the team's thin LB depth. Combined with Devin Bush, this re-signing contributes to fully addressing the LB starter need.

contract.notes: Base $5M + up to $5M in incentives (NLTBE); max value $10M

signings.analysis: Jones re-signs as the primary stopgap at LT with Ozzy Trapilo (patellar tendon) likely out most of 2026. He started 44 games in four seasons but dealt with injuries and inconsistency in 2025. The 1-year structure and incentive-heavy deal signals a bridge — the Bears retain familiarity but are clearly leaving the door open for a draft or trade upgrade at LT. OT remains a significant need for the 2026 draft.

Raymond (a Ben Johnson reunion from Detroit) is a plug-and-play WR4 and return specialist who had 24 catches, 289 yards, and 1 TD with the Lions in 2025, plus 3 return TDs over the last 4 years. He replaces Devin Duvernay's special teams/depth role and partially offsets the Zaccheaus departure. The 1-year deal is explicitly depth — the Bears still need a true WR3 starter. WR need is unchanged in terms of draft priority.

contract.notes: Max value $8M with incentives per Schefter

signings.analysis: Keenum is a player-coach hybrid in the QB room who hasn't thrown a pass in an NFL game since 2023. His value is purely developmental — helping guide Caleb Williams. No need impact. QB was not and is not a need.

contract.notes: Per OverTheCap; $1.225M signing bonus; up to $1M in incentives

signings.analysis: Hardy is an elite special teams contributor (ranked 6th in ST tackles in 2025, the most for any DL) and versatile enough to play SAM linebacker. He is retained as a core special teamer and rotational EDGE, not a pass-rush starter. The re-signing adds depth behind Montez Sweat and partially replaces Dominique Robinson's rotational role. EDGE remains a significant draft need.

contract.notes: 2-year deal per Jeremy Fowler/ESPN; financial terms not yet disclosed

signings.analysis: Lewis is primarily a slot corner/safety hybrid who spent six seasons with the Bills. He has 10 starts over the last two seasons and a 66.0 PFF grade in 2025. He is added to help fill the void left by Nahshon Wright's departure to the Jets, but Lewis is a depth piece — not a Pro Bowl CB1 replacement. CB need remains elevated entering the draft.

contract.notes: Terms undisclosed; expected to be near league minimum or low-cost depth deal per multiple reports

signings.analysis: Street reunites with DC Dennis Allen, who coached him during his best season (3.5 sacks) with the Saints in 2022. He played 7 games off the Falcons' practice squad in 2025 (2 sacks, 6 pressures). He can play DE or DT (three-technique). Pure rotation/depth signing — will compete for a roster spot. EDGE pass rush need remains critical for the draft.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Devin Bush (3yr/$30M, $21M guaranteed) is a legitimate starter coming off a career year. Re-signing D'Marco Jackson (2yr/$7.5M) adds a second credible starter. Combined, the Bears have stabilized a position that lost Edmunds and Ogbongbemiga. T.J. Edwards (fractured fibula) adds uncertainty, so keep LB as a day 3 depth target but it is no longer a premium draft need.

The Bradbury trade (2027 5th for Bradbury) fills the void created by Dalman's retirement with a capable but below-Dalman-tier starter. Bradbury is on an inherited 1-year deal at $5.7M — he is a bridge, not a long-term answer. IOL remains a legitimate draft need, particularly in the mid-rounds, as the Bears should look to upgrade the center position in 2026 or 2027.

Coby Bryant (3yr/$40M, $25.75M guaranteed) is the new S1 and a real investment. However, both Byard (departed/unsigned) and Brisker (expected to depart) leave S2 completely open. The Bears still need a second starting safety. S is a high-priority need but drops from catastrophic (all 4 safeties in free agency) to moderate because one spot is solved.

Braxton Jones re-signed on a 1-year/$5M deal (max $10M with incentives) — this is an explicit bridge with a year-end opt-out. Ozzy Trapilo (patellar tendon) is likely out most of 2026. Jones has anchor issues and a spotty injury history. ESPN described the re-signing as cost-effective but noted it 'doesn't necessarily upgrade the play at the position.' OT remains a top-tier draft priority.

The Bears re-signed Daniel Hardy (special teams depth, not a starter) and added Kentavius Street (rotational, likely near minimum). Dominique Robinson departed to Houston. Montez Sweat remains the only legitimate pass rusher, with Dayo Odeyingbo recovering from Achilles surgery (timeline uncertain). The Bears did NOT sign a legitimate starter at EDGE. This remains the most glaring unaddressed need heading into the draft.

Neville Gallimore (2yr/$12M) is a rotational piece, not a starter — he rarely exceeded 56% snap share in Indy. He replaces Andrew Billings as the new nose tackle but does not upgrade the position meaningfully. Gervon Dexter and Shemar Turner remain the starters. IDL depth is slightly improved but the Bears should still target an impact interior lineman in the draft.

DJ Moore was traded to Buffalo and Zaccheaus departed to Atlanta. Kalif Raymond (1yr/$5.1M) is a WR4/return specialist — he does not fill the WR3 starter void. Rome Odunze and Luther Burden III are expected to handle increased loads, but both are young and on rookie deals. Adding an experienced WR3 starter remains a priority, either late in free agency or in the mid-rounds of the draft.

Nahshon Wright (Pro Bowl, 5 INTs) departed to the Jets. Cameron Lewis (2yr, undisclosed terms) is a reserve CB who is primarily a slot corner/safety hybrid — a depth replacement, not a starter upgrade. Jaylon Johnson remains as CB1. The Bears need a legitimate CB2 for opposite Johnson, making CB a continued mid-round draft priority.

key_additions.headline: Super Bowl champion safety (4 INTs in 2025) fills biggest positional void, becomes new S1
Career-year LB (125 tackles, 2 pick-sixes) replaces Tremaine Edmunds as the defense's new linebacker anchor
Acquired via trade to replace Drew Dalman after shocking retirement; bridge center with 90-start resume
Rotational DT (career-high 3.5 sacks in 2025) replaces Andrew Billings as nose tackle depth
Surprise 2025 starter re-signed after Edmunds release; two-time NFC Defensive Player of the Week in 2025
Bridge LT returns as Ozzy Trapilo (patellar tendon) expected to miss most of 2026
Ben Johnson reunion; WR4 and return specialist (3 return TDs in last 4 years) replaces Zaccheaus/Duvernay depth role
Elite special teamer (led all DL in ST tackles in 2025) retained as core cover unit contributor
Slot corner/safety hybrid adds secondary depth to partially offset Nahshon Wright's departure to the Jets
DC Dennis Allen reunion; rotational DE/DT adds pass-rush depth behind Montez Sweat
Veteran QB mentor retained as player-coach hybrid in Caleb Williams' development room

key_losses.headline: Shocking retirement from Pro Bowl center creates massive IOL void after just one season in Chicago
Most experienced Bear WR traded to Buffalo for second-round pick; leaves Odunze and Burden to lead the room
2025 First-Team All-Pro (NFL-leading 7 INTs) departs, ending a two-year run as Chicago's defensive leader
Released for $15M in cap savings; signed by New York Giants as Bears pivot to younger, faster LB corps
Surprise 2025 Pro Bowler (5 INTs) cashed in and departed, leaving a meaningful hole opposite Jaylon Johnson
Second starting safety departs; Bears lose entire starting safety tandem in one offseason
Rotational EDGE departs, further thinning a pass-rush room that is almost entirely dependent on Montez Sweat
WR3 depth departs for Atlanta reunion; compounds receiver room thinning after DJ Moore trade
Starting nose tackle hits free agency; departure directly triggered Gallimore signing

notes: Contract guarantees for Neville Gallimore (est. $5M) and Cameron Lewis and Kentavius Street (terms not yet publicly reported) are estimated or marked TBD. All other contract figures sourced from ESPN, Bleacher Report, Chicago Sun-Times, Windy City Gridiron, and Over The Cap. Kevin Byard III and Jaquan Brisker are still technically unsigned free agents as of March 11, 2026 — the Bears are widely reported to not be bringing both back, and Brad Biggs of The Score reported the Bears will not re-sign Byard. Treated as departures for needs assessment purposes.
